Nazi Looted Booksat Penn Libraries
Identify a bookHow they were foundBrowse the booksSources & lists
About ▾
ScopeThe methodThe five stepsThe full essay (Bruce Nielsen)What we cannot account forWhere to look
/about/essay/
ScopeThe methodThe five stepsThe full essayWhat we cannot account forWhere to look
The complete essay, in the author’s words

Discovering Nazi Looted books in the Library at the Herbert D. Katz Center for Advanced Judaic Studies, University of Pennsylvania Libraries

Bruce Nielsen, Ph.D. · Judaica Public Services Librarian and Archivist · University of Pennsylvania Libraries
University of Pennsylvania Libraries · Katz Center for Advanced Judaic Studies

Before the end of the Second World War, allied troops had already uncovered thousands of items looted by the Nazis, and much more looted property was uncovered at war’s end. Though not exclusively of Jewish provenance, much of it was; art, torah scrolls, ritual objects, manuscripts and books. Millions of books had been stashed away in basements, salt mines, monasteries, and other hiding places. The books whose former owners were identified were restituted, but several hundred thousand books were heirless, i.e., the former owners could not be identified and thus the books were not restituted but were gathered in warehouses awaiting disposition. With an OAD warehouse stamp and/or a JCR-identifying bookplate (both, to be defined presently) establishing provenance is a straightforward matter. However, for a high percentage of those books there is neither direct OAD nor JCR evidence. Establishing provenance for a great many of these books is an enormous problem and the complexity of the process is formidable; unraveling that complexity is the task at hand.

Our search was to find evidence in the books from the Offenbach Archival Depot (hereafter OAD) and or the Jewish Cultural Reconstruction Inc. (hereafter JCR). OAD was a branch of the US Army’s Monuments, Fine Arts, and Archives section, which ceased operations in April 1949. JCR was the corporation established in April 1947 by the Commission for European Jewish Cultural Reconstruction (a largely American group of Jewish academics, librarians and religious leaders) for the distribution of heirless Jewish property held at OAD. The OAD and JCR were not synonyms nor are the terms interchangeable. What we will attempt in this brief article is to give as accurate and complete a rehearsal of what did or did not happen when books were delivered by JCR to Dropsie College for Hebrew and Cognate Learning. Here we present in some detail all of the kinds of evidence for which we search in order to find and uncover a great many of those books, while at the same time acknowledging the many and painful restarts we endured. With each restart we would encounter another “aha moment,” here is something else to look for and back to the beginning we went.

We are searching for books gifted to Dropsie College for Hebrew and Cognate Learning by the JCR. However, some of the books had different journeys; some were gifts from private individuals, many came in exchange with the Library of Congress, or even with bookdealers. While these exchanged books certainly were looted they should not be counted as among the books shipped to Dropsie by the JCR. All that happened due to the presence of so many exchanged books was to change the project from identifying looted books that were delivered by JCR to Dropsie, to identifying Nazi-looted books no matter how they are sourced. The one thing they all have in common is theft. Every one of these books suffered through three events beginning in the pre-war period when they were in their homes and ending in the period of the late 1940’ and early 1950’s, whether a personal library, or the library of a synagogue or a Jewish organization: they were looted, they were recovered, and they were distributed. For many of them, and probably for the overwhelming majority of them, there was a fourth event; the fact that they had been looted was not marked or indicated in any way and thus, they became non-discoverable; their stories and the memories of past owners were lost and forgotten. The current project will be completed only when the record for each of these cataloged titles has been edited to include a local OAD or JCR provenancial note, and the uncataloged ones are cataloged and they are all discoverable.

There has been a recent upsurge in research surrounding provenancial identification of Nazi-looted materials, an outgrowth of interest generated when President Bill Clinton signed the U.S. Holocaust Assets Commission Act on June 23, 1998. The act established the “Presidential Advisory Commission on Holocaust Assets in the United States,” designed to investigate U.S. assets acquired after 1933, to determine whether any U.S. assets were originally the property of European Jews. The final report is an important document for undertakings such as ours, Plunder and Restitution: Findings and Recommendations of the Presidential Advisory Commission on Holocaust Assets in the United States and Staff Report, December 2000 (PCHA)

https://govinfo.library.unt.edu/pcha/PlunderRestitution.html/html/Home_Contents.html

and specifically, “Chapter VI. Heirless Assets and the Role of Jewish Cultural Reconstruction, Inc.,”

https://govinfo.library.unt.edu/pcha/PlunderRestitution.html/html/StaffChapter6.html. Subsequently the proceedings from a symposium added new information, Confiscation of Jewish Property in Europe, 1933–1945: New Sources and Perspectives Symposium Proceedings (Center for Advanced Holocaust Studies United States Holocaust Memorial Museum, 2003) https://www.ushmm.org/m/pdfs/Publication_OP_2003-01.pdf .

For just one of many recent publications, see Anke Geißler-Grünberg and Lisa Trzaska, NS-Raubgut. Forschungsbericht zur Provenienzforschung an der Universitätsbibliothek Potsdam 2014–2018 (Universitätsverlag Potsdam 2018)

https://www.academia.edu/43926456/Personenrecherche_und_Erbenermittlung

Before we began searching the stacks in the library, we discovered that even knowing how many books we are looking for is not a confirmed fact. According to a JCR document entitled “Distribution of Books in the U.S. from July 1, 1949 to Jan. 31, 1952,” Dropsie received 5549 books processed at OAD, but two different though related Dropsie Library records from the late ‘40’s and early ‘50’s state the number was either 2591 or 2391. This means that, almost from the moment the books and pamphlets were received more than half may never have been acknowledged. Further, in searching the Dropsie institutional archives we have yet to find a list of titles received as requested by the JCR. We strongly caution, however, that this project is not about laying blame or pointing fingers; rather it is about trying to remember identifiable individuals. Might some bureaucratic record keeping have been mishandled or less than rigorously observed? Yes, but the chaotic conditions after the war were palpable throughout American society, let alone dealing directly with plunder that was coming to light all over Germany. Stress, relief, grief all played a role.

Kathy Peiss, the Roy F. and Jeannette P. Nichols Professor of American History at the University of Pennsylvania, demonstrates this same pattern in other libraries. “The LC … a shelf reading … found that 2300 came from Jewish Cultural Reconstruction… (The JCR records indicate that 5708 had been sent to the LC). Many books did not have the JCR bookplate.” “The LC created a virtual library by marking their provenance in the electronic catalogue as Holocaust-Era Judaic Heritage Library,” … According to the LC catalog, there are 1423 volumes in this virtual library.)” This number, 1423, is well below the LC shelf read and a far cry from the number in the JCR records. “The New York Public Library had integrated the 1624 books it received into its general collections, and there is no way to find them. Yale University had 28 entries in its catalog noting books with JCR bookplates, of over 1500 books given the library. Again, “the Jewish Community Library of Los Angeles, which had received over 1,000 JCR books, closed in 2009 and the whereabouts of its 1000 JCR books are unknown.”

From the same Descriptive Catalogue we can cite five additional examples. “Hebrew Union College, Cincinnati, Los Angeles – 9,753 books were originally sent to the Hebrew Union College which merged with the Jewish Institute of Religion in the 1950's. At that time, around 9,380 books were taken over by the Jewish Institute of Religion. Since no note was made within the catalog cards as to the provenance of these items, they are almost impossible to trace at this time. (p. 241).” “Indiana University, Bloomington -- Indiana University originally received 185 JCR books, none of which can be located. (pp. 243-44).” “Boston Hebrew College (formerly Hebrew Teachers College), Newton Centre – in 2010, eight items were found in the library of the Boston Hebrew College <<out of 7,275>>. In an email exchange in spring of 2015, the situation looked more dire: The library is faced with severe financial constraints which resulted in the selling off of parts of the rare book collection. JCR books as well as other books carrying the OAD stamp were part of this rare book collection. In addition, given that there is no (or no surviving) inventory of what was sent to the College by the JCR, as well as no corresponding inventory by the College, it is hard to know what is still within the school’s collection, what was sold off, and what was destined to be buried (p. 242) [incidentally, the Library at the Katz Center acquired one OAD book from the Hebrew Teachers College collection, DS135.G37 J8454 1932; and two rare books without OAD/JCR evidence, PJ5052.S314 T63 1891 rare – PO 301672 FY09 and Z6375. E35 1946 rare – PO 311763 FY09].” “Yiddish Scientific Institute (YIVO), New York – YIVO received 11,681 books. According to a librarian working there, "YIVO indeed has many books with the bookplates of the Jewish Cultural Reconstruction Fund among the YIVO Main stacks collection. The books with JCR bookplates are not located as a separate sub-collection but rather mixed with the other books in the stacks. We don't have a list of books with this JCR bookplate and it's difficult to say how many we have at the moment. All the books are cataloged online, but the catalog records don't contain information on JCR bookplates (p. 242).” “John Hopkins University, New York (sic!) – of the 45 books sent, none could be retrieved. An article in the Boston Globe in 2001 touched upon the subject which initiated research on behalf of John Hopkins University. During that research two volumes bearing a JCR bookplate were found, however, these came to the university through a gift from the Zionist Archives Library (ZAL) (p. 245).”

The situation with the former Dropsie collection is no better than those nine examples. A keyword search of either Jewish Cultural Reconstruction or Offenbach Archival Depot in “Find,” the online catalog at the Penn Libraries, yields 230 matches out of anywhere between 2391-5549 books, but it is actually worse than that. Many if not most of those 230 results are false positives reflecting the fact that many of the records at Dropsie were created by copy cataloging. We checked all of them and while the bibliographical information is correct, clearly the copy catalogers did not remove existing local JCR notes, i.e., from the catalog records of copies held at other institutions.

There is a further layer of forgetting that post-dates the closing of Dropsie in 1986. Namely, when the Annenberg Research Institute (successor to Dropsie) joined the University of Pennsylvania, which did not use the Aleph software system, the University decided to bring the former Dropsie collection into NOTIS, the Penn Libraries online operating system, which did not support vernacular language fonts such as Hebrew. Thus, at some point in 1990’s some tens of thousands of books became non-discoverable by tossing out their Aleph records. Only in the past several years have the majority of these books been identified, and are discoverable in “Find” by title search. Among that large group of books we have found hundreds of OAD/JCR/Nazi-looted books. Worse yet, among that group there is a sizeable number of “problem books” that have not been entered into “Find” and thus are not discoverable – and among the “problems” there are at least several dozen OAD/JCR/Nazi-looted books.

Before going further, here is a brief synopsis of how these thousands of books reached OAD. The overwhelming majority of these books were stolen by one of the two main offices of the NSDAP; the ERR or RSHA. On behalf of Alfred Rosenberg, teams traveled to the occupied territories as a member of the Reichs Leader [Alfred] Rosenberg Taskforce (Einsatzstab Reichsleiter Rosenberg, or, ERR) in order to “sift through” useful documents in libraries, to form the Bibliothek der Institut zur Erforschung der Judenfrage (Institute for Research on the Jewish Question) in Frankfurt. Established in March 1941, the Institute served as the core research library for the planned Central Library of the Hohe Schule (Zentralbibliothek der Hohen Schule, or, ZBHS).

Directly competing with the ERR was The Reich Security Main Office (Reichssicherheitshauptamt, or, RSHA). In 1939 the RSHA was created by combining the Security Service (Sicherheitsdienst, SD) and the Gestapo with Reinhard Heydrich heading the organization. RSHA was an organization subordinate to Heinrich Himmler, head of the Schutzstaffel (SS, Protective Echelon), in which a scientific central library was set up. The library was subordinate to Office VII for “Ideological Research and Evaluation.” Among other things, the library was to create the basis for “enemy research” in the RSHA and to serve the ideological and tactical training of the police and the SS. It consisted of confiscated writings and literature from politically persecuted persons and banned organizations.

In order to regulate responsibilities, an agreement between the ERR, the Gestapo and the Sicherheitsdienst (SD, the intelligence agency of the SS) at the end of July 1940 stipulated that confiscations were “the exclusive right of the security police” and thus of the Reich Security Main Office and would be “sighted and distributed by mutual agreement.”

In February 1949, the U.S. government recognized JCR as the legal trustee of most of the remaining books at OAD when it closed in April 1949. It is uncomplicated to describe what was supposed to have occurred when the participating libraries received a shipment of books from JCR. The JCR stated that the receiving institutions agreed: 1) to adhere a JCR bookplate without obscuring the OAD stamp or any other bookplate, stamp, marking or signature; 2) to provide the JCR with a list of all authors and titles within six months of delivery. As to the disposal of any of these books, the receiving institutions signed an agreement that “no books received may be sold, nor may any be exchanged for other books without the permission of Jewish Cultural Reconstruction obtained prior to the exchange.” The JCR bookplate was to go on the inside of the upper board, the OAD stamp on the flyleaf and/or title page. The OAD stamps are mostly black or red, but there are some purple, blue and green ones as well – does the color of the stamp signify anything? Following local practice, each book was to be sourced in the gutter behind the title page as, “Gift of Jewish Cultural Reconstruction” with a date. Interestingly, a source date by itself helps to determine provenance; for more than four hundred books from the Baltic States, those that are dated are always 1952, and books with a known western European provenance are always dated between 1949 and 1951 – never 1952. Thus, the three forms of evidence are: an OAD stamp, a JCR bookplate and a gutter source.

If a book had been chemically treated for mold, insects or bookworms it should have a stamp on the lower edge of the inside upper board: “desinf. Lk. Sept 45,” desinfektion Landkreis September 1945, or, disinfected in the administrative district in September 1945 (and in only one instance thus far, “desinf. Lk. Okt. 45”). Sometimes that stamp is on the verso of the flyleaf or elsewhere – indicating that bindings were already lacking at the time of treatment. Even in some books with bindings, the stamp is in an unexpected location. Sometimes this stamp is the only OAD/JCR evidence in a book – and in cases of the large number of rebound books, surely some of these stamps were discarded with the old bindings (see below, pp. 11-12).

What about titles that do not conform to the three principle forms of evidence (OAD, JCR, gutter source)? Here we begin to see some of the complexity of the search process. There are many permutations: a book may have only an OAD stamp, only a JCR bookplate, only sourced in the gutter with or without a date, only sourced on the title page with the letters JCR, only sourced in the gutter with the letters JCR. Contrary to the agreements between JCR and Dropsie, in many books JCR bookplates or an OAD or Nazi stamp is covered by an Annenberg Research Institute or University of Pennsylvania bookplate or security tag. Again this flies in the face of, “The management of the JCR was anxious that these traces of memory remain visible.” Luckily, if a stamp was on the flyleaf then in the right light and held at the correct angle, we can often see through the covering bookplate and see the OAD stamp. Frequently, any of these forms of evidence are found in unexpected locations, requiring a level of investigation that was not anticipated when we began. For instance, we found some books in which a JCR bookplate is on the inside of the lower board, and some books in which an OAD stamp is at the rear – initially the need to inspect the rear did not occur to us. We even found a book in which the only evidence is an OAD stamp on the inside of the lower board covered by a Dropsie Library card pocket. Finaly, we found some books in which the only evidence is an OAD stamp on the verso of either the title page or flyleaf or on the table of contents or the verso of the last page. As above, these anomalies resulted in a couple of restarts to our search.

How did we proceed? We employed five different steps during our search in order to ensure as comprehensive results as possible.

1. One by one, we read thousands of cards in the old Dropsie Library card catalog. It is fortuitous that over the years the card catalog was retained because it is in the Freidus Classification Shelf List section of that card catalog that we find the only written record of the source for each of the books, including those sourced as “Gift of Jewish Cultural Reconstruction.” Once again, however, the card catalog records JCR gifts of no more than about nine hundred books, so even here we remain far from the total number. Nearly half of these books do not appear in “Find” and are not on the shelf. A bit more than half of the titles so marked, are discoverable in “Find” and we physically confirmed that they do have some kind of OAD/JCR evidence. However, among this group of books that are in “Find” and on the shelf, are several dozen books marked in the Freidus Classification Shelf List as “gift of Jewish Cultural Reconstruction,” but they do not have any OAD/JCR evidence; for those, the Freidus Classification card is the only JCR evidence. To make things even more problematic, some of the titles in this group were printed after the war, some are sourced to known individuals and some have American ownership markings. Each of these three anomalies raise the possibility that the JCR note on the card was a mistake. And if not a mistake, it calls for some kind of explanation.

2. It is equally fortuitous that a battered handwritten rare manuscripts accession list was retained. Dropsie reportedly received six rare manuscripts, and the only evidence for them is on the accession list. There is no actual evidence on the manuscripts themselves.

3. Based on a JCR Report that Dropsie had received three incunables, we had to physically inspect all of the Dropsie incunables searching for evidence of three incunables that came from JCR.

4. We had to reconnect empty bindings with their textblocks. Many of the JCR books have been rebound and clearly, in some cases, JCR bookplates (and sometimes OAD or Nazi stamps) are lacking because they were discarded with the old bindings. Thus, for example, we found an empty binding, 43 cm, with a JCR bookplate, stamped “desinf. Lk. Sept 1945,” on the spine Nedarim Hb9, inside the upper board “RBR 1739,” and an owner’s autograph on the inside of the lower board. In a hard copy printout of the old Aleph titles, we scanned 73 pages before we found a listing for RBR #1739, LF (large folio), מסכת נדרים מן תלמוד בבלי (Slabodka, 1804). Such an edition does not appear in Vinograd, Steinschneider or Van Straalen; Vinograd records only one Nedarim printed in 1804 and that was from Slavuta, Ukraine. In the Rare Book Room there is a volume of the same size; 44 cm, Nedarim Nazir (Slaṿitah, Ukraine: Dov ber ben Yiśrael, 1804), on the title page in pencil, “Hb 9.” It was rebound and does not have a JCR bookplate and no OAD or Nazi stamp. Thus, we originally did not include it in our database. Not until we found the empty binding, and upon re-examining the volume in the Rare Book Room did we recognize the same owner’s autograph (verso of last page). Another example of an empty binding, 33 cm, marking on the spine reads מגן אבות Ka S36, which has a JCR bookplate but no stamps. In the stacks of the Library, there is one book already in our database, Magen Avot, v. 2, 34cm, with a JCR bookplate but not sourced, and on the spine Ka S36. Volume 1 c. 1 is rebound, and has no JCR bookplate and no OAD or Nazi stamps. The combination of the classification Ka S36 and the fact that the thickness of the textblock matches this empty binding proves that volume 1 also belongs in the database.

5. By far the most time-consuming and tedious step was a shelf read of the entire collection. That said, even the shelf read was problematic due to labeling issues. For obviously we should not have needed to inspect books printed after the war, but unfortunately, we found books for which the date on the spine was post-war (i.e., no need to pull it off the shelf) but the title page has a pre-1941 date of publication – and the volume has OAD/JCR evidence. This resulted in our having to restart the search and include books with spine dates through 1948.

When we reached the stage of the shelf read we expected to find books with all three forms of evidence. What we quickly discovered, however, is that a book could have all three major forms of evidence (an OAD stamp, a JCR bookplate, and gutter source), or only one or two. In addition to any OAD/JCR evidence, a book may have one of more than a dozen different Nazi stamps. A title might have just one of the three forms of evidence plus a Nazi stamp. A title may have only a Nazi stamp, but that is sufficient to establish that it had been looted.

Finally, a special Nazi marking in a Hebrew book is a pastedown, usually on the inside upper board, with a handwritten transliteration of title, author and publication information by Johannes Pohl, special assistant to Alfred Rosenberg at the ERR. Sometimes it is clear that Pohl’s pastedown has been torn out and sometimes there is just a tiny fragment of what might have been Pohl’s pastedown. Pohl was a Catholic priest, who among his studies attended the Pontifical Biblical Institute in Rome. He worked in the Oriental Department of the Prussian State Library as a Hebraist from 1935 to 1936 and from 1939 to 1941. He joined the NSDAP in 1940, and was dismissed from his clerical position. In January 1942, Pohl was involved in the robbery and destruction of Jewish cultural assets in Vilnius. He had the most valuable books and manuscripts stolen from the Vilnius museums and libraries. He then selected a small fraction for the planned museum in Frankfurt. He sold the rest to a paper mill.

One sees again, that even with five clear steps, twists and turns are abundant, the cumulative effect of which is to add to the complexity of the search.

Beginning at the end of 1945 the American Joint Distribution Committee (AJDC) was instrumental in sending books to the DP camps in the American Zone in Germany, for daily use and study by the Jewish refugees. Approximately 20,000 books were “borrowed” from OAD with very little if any record keeping. Each book was stamped with, “A.J.D.C. LIBRARY NOT TO BE REMOVED FROM THE PREMISES.” In particular, it was difficult to track books forwarded to DP camps that were subsequently dismantled or lay outside the American Zone. A book bearing an AJDC Library stamp with some form of OAD/JCR/Nazi evidence is easy to identify as looted, but without some OAD or Nazi evidence it is complicated because some AJDC books were donated to the DP camps by various Jewish organizations such as the Jewish Labor Committee, the World Jewish Congress, and the YIVO Institute. If the book has a personal stamp that appears on one of the lists, it certainly came through OAD.

There are many books that have no OAD, JCR or Nazi evidence, but they have bookplates and/or stamps from mostly German Jewish institutional libraries (synagogue, school, communal organization) known to have been looted and destroyed by the Nazis. There are some books with these stamps or bookplates that also have some form of OAD/JCR/Nazi evidence, indicating it is possible a book with no evidence and not sourced at all may have come through OAD. Some of these books are sourced to JCR in the gutter behind the title page, some are sourced as purchased – and therefore excluded from our database – and some are not sourced at all. If a volume is not sourced at all and was printed after, say, 1925, there is a much greater chance it came through OAD than somehow getting to Dropsie pre-war. There are multiple digital lists of such institutional libraries and any given library may show up on multiple lists. Here are the digital resources for tracking these libraries:

31 March 1946, 67 “Institutional Library Collections – Germany,” without the number of books processed from each of them https://www.fold3.com/image/270207612

31 July 1946, 35 institutional Jewish libraries in Germany with the number of books processed from each of them, total 33,499 https://www.fold3.com/image/232711619

30 Sep 1946 OAD list of 582 Jewish libraries in Germany with the number of books processed from each of them, total 58,667 https://www.fold3.com/image/232711733

RSHA - Reichssicherheitshauptamt. Bibliothek, lists dozens of synagogues and schools without numbers of books https://provenienz.gbv.de/Deutsches_Reich._Reichssicherheitshauptamt._Bibliothek .

A book may have no direct JCR evidence and may not be sourced at all (purchase, exchange, gift), but it might have a personal stamp and/or an autograph of a person whose name appears on one of many lists of victims murdered by the Nazis. Often times in the book there is a geographical locator as well, and many of the lists include locations for the personal names. For a book with such a personal stamp and locator, if it was printed say, after 1925, it seems very unlikely to have made it to Dropsie pre-war. It is much more likely that it was looted and the only question is, did it go through OAD and/or was it delivered by JCR? In order to be exhaustive we consulted all relevant lists. Even if a book is sourced as having been purchased or exchanged during the war and it has a known name, clearly it did not pass through OAD and was not delivered by JCR, but we must consider the likelihood it was looted. Again, a more recent date of publication makes it more difficult to imagine that it arrived in the U.S pre-war. A name frequently appears on more than one of these lists, but some names appear only on a single list. Adding to the complexity of dealing with multiple lists is the fact that many of them are open-ended, being added to and edited at the current time.

OAD Monthly Reports https://www.fold3.com/image/232711531

“List of the Privately-owned (identifiable) Book Collections” stored at the Central Collecting Point Wiesbaden, October 1949, www.fold3.com/image/232025073

“Looted Cultural Assets,” https://lootedculturalassets.de/

“Tentative List of Jewish Cultural Treasures in Axis-Occupied Countries,” https://www.lootedart.com

“German Lost Art Foundation,” https://www.lostart.de/en/found/object/das-reichsbeamten-mit-nebengesetzen-textausgabe-mit-verweisungen-und/524456

Stolpersteine Project, https://www.stolpersteine.eu/en/home/

Holocaust Memorial to the Murdered Jews of Europe: https://www.holocaust-denkmal-berlin.de/raum-der-namen/biographien/

Pages on Nazi loot, Common Library Network, German https://provenienz.gbv.de/Kategorie:Raubgut

Claims Conference; Memorial Book. Victims of the Persecution of Jews under the National Socialist Tyranny in Germany 1933 – 1945, https://www.bundesarchiv.de/gedenkbuch/en1041664 , or, http://www.bundesarchiv.de/gedenkbuch/directory.html

Jewish genealogy website Holocaust database, https://www.jewishgen.org/databases/Holocaust/

U.S Holocaust Memorial Museum – Holocaust Survivors and Victims Database, https://www.ushmm.org/online/hsv/person_advance_search.php

“Conference on Jewish Material Claims against Germany” https://www.haaretz.co.il/st/inter/Hheb/images/listofnames.pdf

Database of Dutch victims of the Nazis, https://www.joodsmonument.nl/https://www.joodsmonument.nl/

Yad Vashem the Central Database of Shoah Victims' Names https://yvng.yadvashem.org/

Commemorating places Jews had lived, https://kehilalinks.jewishgen.org/

https://blog.hamapah.org/searchable-map-of-hebrew-place-names/

many entries have a list of residents, e.g., list of 848 Jews residing in Butrimonys, Lithuania in the year 1937; https://kehilalinks.jewishgen.org/Butrimonys/1937%20Butrimonys%20Jewish%20Residents.htm .

Turkish born Sephardim deported from France during WWII

http://www.sephardicstudies.org/pdf/turks.pdf

Combined Surnames List – Izmir brides and grooms, Turkey deportees, etc.

https://www.sephardicgen.com/databases/IndexSearchEngine.php?=&offset=175201

NS-Documentation Center of the City of Cologne https://museenkoeln.de/ns-dokumentationszentrum/default.aspx?s=315

Bayerischer Lehrer-und Lehrerinnenverband e.V.

https://www.bllv.de/projekte/geschichte-bewahren/erinnerungsarbeit/datenbank-jued-lehrer/datenbank

http://jdcrp.org/documentation-of-persecuted-jewish-collectors/

Dienststelle des Reichsleiter Rosenberg in den westlichen

https://www.errproject.org/docs/looted_libraries_nl/1a.pdf

https://www.errproject.org/looted_libraries_fr_lists.php

Beyond a name on a list, it is also necessary, when possible, to check a stamp itself to confirm the correct identification. The most useful database is the digitized version of the two-volume set of library stamps arranged by Isaac Bencowitz during his time as head of Offenbach, May to November 1946:

Library Markings found among the looted books in the Archival Depot, vol. I, Eastern, https://www.nli.org.il/en/archives/NNL_ARCHIVE_AL003531216/NLI ,

Library Markings found among the looted books in the Archival Depot, vol. II, Western,

https://www.nli.org.il/en/archives/NNL_ARCHIVE_AL003925829/NLI .

See also a useful summary of the various stamps by Michael Krupp, “Schicksal von Büchern,” Das Gesamte (2014), https://lee-achim.de/html/i-faith/rii1412.pdf .

What about books that came from the Baltic and neighboring states? There are many with a known personal name but no OAD, JCR or Nazi evidence. Why, out of more than four hundred books mostly from Lithuania, are there only two with an OAD stamp; none with a Nazi stamp? There are several digital resources for tracking eastern European victims of the Nazis, which need to be consulted in each case. Lists and resources for communities often include lists of pre-war residents. The most prominent digital resources are:

OAD Monthly Reports https://www.fold3.com/image/232711531

“List of the Privately-owned (identifiable) Book Collections” stored at the Central Collecting Point Wiesbaden, October 1949, www.fold3.com/image/232025073

“Partial List of Holocaust Martyrs of Bialystok,” http://www.zchor.org/bialystok/yizkor-ma.htm

Database of victims in Czechoslovakia, https://www.holocaust.cz/en/main-2/

Database of Polish surnames, https://jri-poland.org/

Yad Vashem the Central Database of Shoah Victims' Names https://yvng.yadvashem.org/

Liste der am 14. November 1941 aus Berlin in das Ghetto Minsk deportierten Jüdinnen und Juden

http://zeitzeugenarchiv.gwminsk.com/sites/default/files/spisok_evreev_deportirovannyh_14_noyabrya_1941_goda_iz_berlina_v_minskoe_getto.pdf

The Jews of Posen Province

https://www.wbc.poznan.pl/Content/381538/PDF/Jews%20of%20Posen%20Province.pdf

Geneanet – Jüdische Familien aus Deutschland

https://gw.geneanet.org/pfdm?lang=en&pz=israel&nz=fleischmann&ocz=1&m=P&v=X&t=A

Index to the Case Files of the AJDC Emigration Service, Prague Office, 1945-1950

https://archives.jdc.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/07/AJDC-Prague-Emigration-Case-File-Index.pdf

Josef Rosin, Protecting Our Litvak Heritage. A History of 50 Jewish Communities in Lithuania (Coral Gables, Florida: Friends of the Yurburg Jewish Cemetery, 2009)

https://www.jewishgen.org/yizkor/lithuania6/lithuania6.html#TOC , or,

https://www.jewishgen.org/yizkor/lithuania6/files/Lithuania6.pdf

Josef Rosin, Preserving Our Litvak Heritage – Volume II. A History of 21 Jewish Communities in Lithuania (League City, TX: JewishGen, 2007), https://www.jewishgen.org/yizkor/lithuania5/lithuania5.html

Nancy and Stuart Schoenburg, Lithuanian Jewish Communities (New York : Garland Pub., 1991)

https://ia801602.us.archive.org/19/items/nybc314248/nybc314248.pdf

The Holocaust in 21 Lithuanian towns, compiled and translated by Joe Woolf, https://www.jewishgen.org/yizkor/lithuania3/Lithuania3.html#TOC

JewishGen Yizkor Book Project https://www.jewishgen.org/yizkor/

https://www.jewishgen.org/databases/Holocaust/

From chapter 6 of the PCHA, see above p. 3, “Books from the Baltic States:”

Certain library and archival collections that the Nazis looted from the Baltic States were recovered by U.S. forces and stored at collecting points such as the OAD. This property was not restituted to these countries for several reasons. The United States did not recognize the status of the Baltic States as Soviet Republics. In addition, very few Jews remained in the area; the Nazis had murdered practically the entire community. As of September 1948, the OAD held about 20,000 books from Jewish, and about 8,000 books from non-Jewish, institutions in the Baltic States. Among other things, the OAD housed the papers of the Yiddish Scientific Institute (YIVO), formerly of Vilnius, Lithuania, a collection whose size was estimated to be over 50,000 "items," including documents, sheet music, brochures, and newspapers. By mid-1947, these had already been shipped to YIVO, which had re-established itself in the United States.

The disposition of the remaining “items” from the Baltic States remained in doubt. The matter was discussed in May 1946, not only in the State-War-Navy Coordinating Committee, which was considering transfer of the property to “representative international Jewish groups,” but also by the Joint Chiefs of Staff. By July 22, 1949, the JCR was given access to the Baltic material even though the owners of most of the materials could be identified. By September, the JCR had shipped 214 cases with about 29,000 books, 78 of them (12,418 books) to Israel. The 136 remaining cases (16,346 books), which contained mostly identifiable volumes, were shipped to the warehouses of the AJDC in Paris, for a two-year trusteeship during which identifiable books could be claimed. As part of its obligation, the JCR attempted to locate the owners of these books by, “advertising in the centers of the Jewish world the names (16,000) of the owners,” though not if they owned less than five books. In 1951, after the two years' trusteeship ended, the JCR proposed to send all remaining Baltic books to Israel, since they contained, “chiefly rabbinical and other Hebrew literature of a rather high quality.” However, “innumerable requests from libraries in the United States” for this type of material, as well as from Western Europe, prompted the Board to vote instead to distribute the remaining Baltic collection according to the 40:40:20 ratio to Israel, the Western Hemisphere, and all other countries.

How far can we push speculation about a book having been looted without any direct evidence? There is a small group of twenty-nine Yiddish and Hebrew titles, all from one of seven eastern European cities (Moscow, Minsk, Kiev, Kharkov, Vilna, Kaunas, and Warsaw), all printed between 1912 and 1941 (only seven before 1929) and all with the same Library of Congress stamp and date when they were received.

Seventeen of them are marked as “exchange – Library of Congress” on several different dates between 1953 and 1959 (eight are sourced but sans a date; eleven are not sourced at all). None of them has any direct OAD, JCR; though one has the AJDC Library stamp on the rear cover. Two of them have a LoC Surplus Duplicate stamp on the front cover. We highly suspected they were received at the LoC as a distinct batch of material from the Baltic States with the code 0251K being a geographical indicator. The date and places of publication made it reasonable to suspect a JCR provenance.

When we searched the LoC online catalog for each of these titles, we found that eleven of them have the following local note: “Copy 1 of this book was presented to the Library of Congress by Jewish Cultural Reconstruction, Inc … It was added to the collection on Sept 05, 1950.” Those eleven catalog records also have the designation, Holocaust-Era Judaic Heritage Library; but there is no indication that a JCR bookplate was adhered. One title in this group appears in the LoC catalog with the following local note: “Copy 1 of this book bears the book stamp, “Sichergestellt durch Einsatzstab RR Reval” and was acquired by the LoC through a federal transfer in the immediate aftermath of the Second World War,” and it has the designation, Holocaust-Era Judaic Heritage Library. The presence of this stamp strongly suggests the provenance for all of the other books in this group. The Katz Center copy of this title, by David Mitzmacher, Di kapele: dertseylungen (Moscow, 1941), does not have the Einsatzstab stamp which proves the LoC received multiple copies of some, if not all of these titles, and the numeral 6 on the stamp may indicate 6th copy. Twelve of the twenty-nine are in the LoC online catalog but lack the local note and designation, and one of them does not appear in the catalog. It seems highly likely that these twenty-nine items all arrived at the LoC in one shipment from the JCR. We must imagine the following scenario occurred. There were multiple copies of each title and copy 1 remained at LoC as currently reflected by the online catalog. In a series of exchanges over the next nine years, Dropsie received copies of these titles. As we saw with the Freidus Classification Shelf List, the LoC catalog records are the only OAD, JCR or Nazi evidence for these titles. Again, as we wrote above concerning “exchange” books, these titles were looted and were part of the Dropsie collection, but they should not be counted among the titles that came from JCR to Dropsie.

Similarly, there is an additional cause for speculation that a book was looted without any direct evidence. This arises in cases in which we found only an incomplete multi-volume set and one or more volumes have OAD/JCR evidence. For instance, how can we explain the fact that of a six part set, part one and even a second copy of it, has OAD/JCR evidence but parts two and three do not, and parts four-six are lacking entirely? What is the probability that Dropsie had a copy of parts two & three bound together, another copy of part three but lacked parts one and four through six, and remarkably JCR added two copies of part one? It is far easier to understand that all the copies came from JCR and the only ones marked were the two copies of part one, than to imagine only two copies of part one were added to a pre-existing broken set. Similarly, given a full four consecutive-years run of a Russian language newspaper, 208 issues housed in individual folders, how can we explain why only ten issues have an OAD stamp? It is far more difficult to imagine that Dropsie held 198 issues and precisely the ten issues lacking were supplied from JCR, than it is to understand that they all came through OAD.

In summation, what we originally thought would be, although tedious, a straightforward and simple search has turned out to be anything but simple. Complications, anomalies and variations beyond what we could have imagined when we began have caused restarting our search more than once. We hope that having laid out the processes in somewhat detailed fashion that other libraries will be encouraged to search their collections for Nazi looted materials and to make them discoverable.

We have not finished the search in the Library at the Katz Center, but we are getting close. What we can say at this time is the total number of titles we have discovered by all the steps outlined above is more than 2,100 out of a stated but never confirmed estimated total of 2391-5549 titles. When our shelf read is completed, there are so many stories that we need to raise up, individuals to be recognized, and memories honored.

Provenance research & scholarship by Bruce Nielsen, Ph.D. — Judaica Public Services Librarian & Archivist, Penn Libraries (Katz Center for Advanced Judaic Studies)A site built by Judaica DH at Penn Libraries
